perturbation.original_text: Wildform shall perform the Transition Services for which it is responsible hereunder following commonly accepted standards of care in the industry and exercising the same degree of care as it exercises in performing the same or similar services for its own business as of the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Wildform shall endeavor to perform the Transition Services for which it is responsible hereunder, making efforts to align with industry practices, while considering the level of care it applies to its own business operations as of this Agreement's date.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'shall perform...following commonly accepted standards of care' with 'shall endeavor to perform...making efforts to align with industry practices'. This introduces ambiguity as 'endeavor' and 'making efforts' are subjective and do not guarantee adherence to industry standards. Furthermore, the original text requires Wildform to exercise the same degree of care as it exercises in performing the same or similar services for its own business as of the date of this Agreement, while the modified text requires them to only 'consider' the level of care. This weakens the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text mandates adherence to industry standards, which aligns with the implied warranty of merchantability. The modified text weakens this obligation, potentially allowing Wildform to provide substandard services. If these services negatively impact On2's business, it could be argued that Wildform is engaging in unfair competition by failing to meet reasonable industry standards, violating California Business and Professions Code § 17200, which prohibits unfair or fraudulent business acts or practices and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to adhere to industry standards, potentially leading to substandard services. The original text mandated adherence to industry standards. The altered text replaces 'shall perform...following commonly accepted standards of care' with 'shall endeavor to perform...making efforts to align with industry practices'. This introduces ambiguity. California Business and Professions Code § 17200 defines unfair competition as any 'unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By weakening the obligation to meet industry standards, Wildform could be seen as engaging in an unfair business practice if their services negatively impact On2's business, thus creating a contradiction with BPC 17200.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Wildform receives such Nonconformance Notice from On2, Wildform shall make commercially reasonable efforts to modify such Deliverable and re-deliver it to On2 after any such modification is completed.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Wildform receives such Nonconformance Notice from On2, Wildform will attempt to modify such Deliverable and re-deliver it to On2 when modifications are possibly complete.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'shall make commercially reasonable efforts' with 'will attempt to modify', making the obligation less stringent. The phrase 'when modifications are possibly complete' is vague and ambiguous, leaving room for subjective interpretation. This weakens the obligation to correct non-conformities in a timely and effective manner.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause mandates 'commercially reasonable efforts' to rectify non-conforming deliverables. This provides a benchmark tied to industry standards. The modified language weakens this obligation, and makes the delivery contingent on a subjective possibility rather than a defined standard, creating non-compliance with California Commercial Code § 2314, which implies a warranty that goods are merchantable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall make commercially reasonable efforts' with 'will attempt to modify' and uses the vague phrase 'when modifications are possibly complete.' This weakens the obligation to correct non-conformities. The law explanation argues that this creates non-compliance with California Commercial Code § 2314, which implies a warranty that goods are merchantable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section 2314 (1), states that 'Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind.' By weakening the obligation to correct non-conformities, the modified text arguably undermines this implied warranty of merchantability, creating a contradiction. The original contract provided a more concrete standard ('commercially reasonable efforts'), aligning with the implied warranty, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Wildform agrees not to cause or permit the porting, reverse engineering, reverse assembly or reverse compilation of any object code software provided to Wildform by On2 pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Wildform should avoid causing or permitting the porting, reverse engineering, reverse assembly or reverse compilation of any object code software provided to Wildform by On2 pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'agrees not to cause or permit' to 'should avoid causing or permitting', the obligation is weakened from a strict prohibition to a suggestion. 'Should avoid' is not legally binding and provides Wildform with leeway. This modification introduces a legal ambiguity concerning the restrictions on reverse engineering.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) 17 U.S. Code § 1201
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text strictly prohibits reverse engineering, which, depending on the specific implementation, may align with the DMCA's prohibition on circumventing technological measures that effectively control access to a copyrighted work. By weakening the prohibition to 'should avoid,' this creates ambiguity. Should Wildform engage in reverse engineering, even if 'avoiding' it was only a suggestion, it could be interpreted as a violation of the DMCA's anti-circumvention provisions, depending on the software's protective measures and the nature of the reverse engineering activity.

Circumvention of copyright protection systems (a) Violations Regarding Circumvention of Technological Measures .-(1)(A) No person shall circumvent a technological measure that effectively controls access to a work protected under this title. The prohibition contained in the preceding sentence shall take effect at the end of the 2-year period beginning on the date of the enactment of this chapter. (B) The prohibiti
perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees not to cause or permit' to 'should avoid causing or permitting' weakens a strict prohibition to a mere suggestion. This introduces a legal ambiguity regarding reverse engineering. According to 17 U.S.C. 1201 (a)(1)(A), 'No person shall circumvent a technological measure that effectively controls access to a work protected under this title.' The original text aligns with this by strictly prohibiting reverse engineering. The modified text, however, weakens this prohibition, potentially allowing Wildform to engage in reverse engineering with less legal consequence, thereby contradicting the intent of 17 U.S.C. 1201, which aims to prevent circumvention of copyright protection systems.
